sentences.text: Oh, well, I had a beautiful doll, which I received from America.
And that was-- it had a porcelain face, and nobody saw a beautiful doll like that in our little town.
Yeah, from, from the United States, yes.
From my relatives.
My mother's brother and two sisters, Bella?
Lena?
Ah, two sisters came to the United States before World War I.
Oh, yes.
My-- the oldest sister was Berta, or we called her Bella and then Lena, and then came my mother's brother, Adolf, and my mother was the youngest.
So there were--
I do.
My Aunt Bell was born Gelb, and she became Yagoda.
And her last married name was Smith.
And my aunt Lena, who lived in New York, was born Gelb, and she be-- I'm sorry.
She was born Gelb.
That's right.
And she became Parness when she married Uncle Louis.
And Uncle Adolf was born Gelb, and he changed his name to Gero.
G-E-R-O.
It's an O with two commas on top.
And he, he lived in Budapest, at first.
He studied there, and then he came to the United States.
He was an orthopedic shoe specialist, and he did work for disabled people throughout the country.
We corresponded through the mail.
I remembered their addresses by heart, actually.
So, it, it-- later I would like to explain the connection, how important this became to me.
I had a doll and a little carriage that they sent me.
And I remember so proudly pushing the little carriage around and sleeping with the doll and combing her hair.
I also joined a, a small group, and we put on little plays.
[LAUGHS] It was nothing sophisticated, I assure you.
I can't even remember some of the plays.
But our little group has disintegrated when the key organizer came to the United States with her-- [LAUGHS] with her family, and to the state of New York.
And so, after that, we broke up.
As I mentioned, I also sang in a-- in a choir group.
Again, it wasn't sophisticated, but it was great fun.
I enjoyed going to the library and look at books.
The library was not very large.
In fact, it was just the opposite-- very small.
I almost finished all the books that they had there.

They really didn't have very many books, and I especially used to complain that there are no pictures in them, which I looked forward-- I looked forward to whenever I could put my fingers on some of these.
Oh, I liked-- I liked stories.
I liked [NON-ENGLISH], for example.
There's a whole series about this man called [NON-ENGLISH].
And it was funny, and I would laugh.
For example, he would go to Budapest from a small town, and I envisioned myself going to Budapest from our little small town.
And he, he's-- he-- in the book, his impressions about this-- this big city were, were they put people on top of the, [LAUGHS] on top of the roofs, and they look down upon him.
Of course, he was talking about the statues.
And-- and he was always afraid that they were going to fall on him.
And it was so funny, just reading about it.
Not as a child.
As an adult, yes.
Well, at age 14, when I was taken out of high school to-- because we were shipped to Auschwitz, at that point I had nine, ten-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seven.
I don't know where to start from because I went to kindergarten, and I went to nursery school.
And from one-- from first to seventh grade, I was in elementary school.
And then I-- then I was sent to high school because I had good grades.
And this was in Ber-- Bereg-- Beregszasz.
From Berehy-- from north Berehy to Beregszasz.
And, and I was there for two years.
And-- well, it was one month short of it, but I'll call it two years.
It was still in elementary school when, when the Hungarians came in.
Actually, not initially, but once we had to close our business.
But the year after the Hungarians came in-- they came in 1938.
In 1939, all Jews had to close their stores.
And this created a tremendous hardship, eventually, on those Jews, particularly, who had no fields, as we had.
Some Jews had depended on their income entirely, on the little mom and pop store that they operated.
And once the savings ran out, and later, even the savings became frozen and the-- and people, or Jews could not touch their savings.
So it was really up to the Jewish community to help them, see them through.
We helped a lot with milk and with the products that we grew in the fields-- grains, corn, potatoes, grapes, wine.
And, and so we were able to help.
We had a very large family, but most of them owned land-- most of them.
But we had other relatives who did not.
And all the Jews in our town were close friends.
Yes, of course.
Oh yes.
In 1938, I was eight years old.
I, I noticed that we were not as popular anymore as we used to be, and our-- eventually our closest non-Jewish friends had ignored us.
And this wasn't always the case, but it happened more and more frequently as time went by and anti-Semitism set in.
They started singing anti-Semitic songs, and these, these were usually words, anti-Semitic words, to existing melodious melodies.
So that, as children, we just wanted to sing the melody without paying too much attention to the harmful words to the song.
I remember one time, I came home and it could have been my sister, but I remember mother's reaction.
She said, what are you singing there?
So, I said, oh, the new song, and it made fun of the Jews.
You remember that?
Oh, yes.
[SINGING IN HUNGARIAN] Whoever has a Jewish girl for a girlfriend, [HUNGARIAN].
That means that should tie a rope around his or her neck.
These are-- but the-- you can hear the melody.
It's, it's very-- I started too low.
I'm a little bit hoarse right now.
But there were other songs, and my sister and I, when we get together, we, we remember these times.
How was that affecting the children in your school, the other children toward you, non-Jewish children?
Well, naturally, we were not more popular, as you can well understand.
And soon, we were isolated.
We played dances.
We would-- we would stand in a large circle and somebody would pick up someone for a dance, and if you were Jewish, you weren't picked up.
You were not asked to be-- to dance.
And this may seem very unimportant and harmless, but as children, you feel hurt when you realize that you are no longer popular, and only because you are a Jew.
And it was bewildering, confusing, degrading.
Well, our parents said that we should-- we should stay with Jewish kids, that perhaps it will be safer because sometimes Jewish boys were beaten up.
Girls-- I haven't heard in our town about girls, but boys got into fights because they fought back.
They were taught to defend themselves and protect themselves.
But I was not taught to fight back, and girls just weren't.
We had-- we could tell each other off, verbally, but we would not attack each other.
I, I have never seen that in my town.
I'm sure it must have happened, but I, I don't recollect any such event.
And of course, as time went on, we had to start wearing the yellow star.

Only because my father was a hero during World War I in the Austria-Hungarian empire, and he had six medals to show for it, he was excused from-- he and his family was excused from wearing the yellow star.

And there is a document that I made a copy of that my brother had-- and I will be glad to share that with you-- which states that David Hollander and his family are excused from wearing the yellow star because he was a hero in World War I, as you know, fighting on the German side.
And what's interesting about this is that, that-- we have a picture.
And what's interesting about this is that, that, in World War I, they fought on the side of the Germans.
And of course, they lost.
But then came World War II, and both my father and his brothers were taken to the Holocaust.
And my father is the sole survivor of that Holocaust.
His brothers and his sister were all gassed in Auschwitz upon arrival, and most of their children.
So here, in one war, they were the heroes.
In another war, they were subhumans.
Well, I, I know that it started much before, or long before I became aware of it because Jewish men started disappearing.
They-- eventually, their wives would receive a letter that they died on the-- on the Russian front, all of them.
But word tickled down-- trickled down that they were murdered.
And people didn't know how this happened, and we could sometimes imagine that somebody just took a gun and, and shot them-- shot them at random.
I don't know how it worked.
This is not-- I am not an eyewitness to that, but, but I know my uncle disappeared that way, leaving behind six children.
And there were many in our town who disappeared that way, including our shohet.
So today, even to date, we, we can only conjecture how they died.
Later, much later, the men talked about-- after the Holocaust-- a few men, that is, who returned, talked about how these people were taken out, and they were-- they were shot into the river.
But I never heard that when I was home, so I hesitate talking about something that I did not witness because I do have a lot of eyewitness accounts to tell at this filming.
Well, we were-- it began in '38.
And we kept our cattle.
We kept our house.
We were-- we lived as we normally did, except that it became abnormal because the income from the store wasn't there.
The, the-- our entire lives totally shifted to the fields.
That became a godsend to us.
Some of this, we inherited on both sides-- my mother's side and my father's side of the families.
And some of it, my dad purchased.
And I have a little list which my father gave me in which he-- he wrote himself-- in which he very interestingly names all the fields that we had.
And until then, I could only remember first seven, then 14, and then I knew there was more.
I couldn't-- and later my dad was able to write down every one of them and how big they-- the size of the fields, and how he obtained them, how he purchased them.
That, that one was because someone couldn't pay the mortgage and the bank had to take it over.
And it went up for sale, and anybody could buy it.
And here my dad saved money in order to purchase more and more land so that we can grow, along with the size of the family.
I know that my parents and grandparents were born in this town, so far as I know.
But beyond that I don't know.
So I-- this would take me to about the middle of the 19th century, 18-- maybe 1840, 1850.
Well, in 1944, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the anti-Semitism reached its climax.
The German-- I'd like to go back a step.
After the Hungarians took over in 1938, the Hungarians continued the anti-Semitic laws that, that were put into effect in Germany.
They filtered down into the German occupied areas.
Well, it filtered down into our town, and that's what I am here to report about.
And so the drummer would come, and he'd stop at the corners of the streets.
And people would drop everything they were doing to listen to the news.
And the announcements came through a drummer first.
Those who received newspapers didn't necessarily get it for a week.
So by the time you received your newspaper, it was already ancient history, so fast did these laws move.
One day, we had to give up our store.
Next day, we couldn't be out any more beyond a certain time.
If, if we were caught after that, we would be shot.
At onl-- this was applicable only to Jews, and of course, something must be wrong with the Jews is the message that the-- that the population got.
And so, it spread like wild-- wildfire.
And we eventually became afraid.
Although nothing happened to, to us, there were incidents which my brothers have faced.
Now--
Well, there were no guns, but my brothers had to obtain for them-- I don't know.
They're called steel something that they put on their fingers, made out of some sort of metal.
And that if they are attacked, that they should have this in their pockets, put them on quickly, and hit back so that they-- to scare them off.
Well, the anti-Semites.
The anti-Semites.
The-- usually their peer group.
The old folks generally were not anti-Semitic.
It was-- we lived peacefully with the non-Jews.
It was the reformed church that was basically the, the key religion in our town.
It's called reformatus.
I don't know how to put it in English.
We had one reformed church.
And we had to take our exam, I just recalled, in this church.
Our final exams were taken in the church orally.
I-- and it, it-- obviously, it made you feel that you don't belong in this building to begin with.
And secondly, you felt that whenever you were asked a question, it was so-- the question was placed in such a way or given in such a way that it completely melted me.
I, I remember, I would completely-- what's the word?
I just couldn't think of the answer.
I was so scared, I-- as if I was surrounded by the German Army, and the German army was not even there yet.
This was in elementary school, soon after the Hungarians came in.
To move on now, one morning, we had a knock on the door, and that we should be ready to leave in a half an hour.
There were Hungarian gendarme and German SS, four people in all.
And we should turn the keys over to them.
However, the night before, my father hear-- my father's friend came over in the middle of the night to tell my dad that tomorrow morning, the Nazis would round up all the Jews and take them all away.
And it was in-- we were taken away on April 15th, so it must have been on the 14th, but I don't-- about dates, just allow me to go-- to go loosely here, please.
In 1944, definitely.
Shortly-- a day before.
So my father told my brothers to go and see so-and-so and so-and-so and so-and-so and tell them that-- what will happen in the morning, and come right back.
And they did, and they weren't even allowed to be outside at night because of the curfew.
They came back, and that they should also notify others.
In other words, to spread the word.
There were-- there were no telephones in private homes yet at this time.
Then, my father called us together and said that we have to leave tomorrow morning, and we don't know where we are going, but that we will hide the family jewelry, which was our investment.
And so, that we should all know the hiding places, these-- that this would-- we can always turn this jewelry into cash when we would come back home.
But what we didn't know is that we might never return.

That night, my father and my brothers dug a hole under the hardwood floor in one of our bedrooms, and they placed a metal box under this hardwood floor, and replaced the hardwood as naturally as it was possible.
On top of it was furniture, and actually, it was a wash stand that was on top of it, with towels.
And sure enough, at 5:00 in the morning came the knock on the door.
So we were-- all-- we all had to go to city hall where already other Jewish families were waiting.
When all the Jews were rounded up in [? north ?] Berehy.
We were driven in a truck to the neighboring city called Beregszasz, the provincial capital.
At first, we were taken to the beautiful synagogue in Beregszasz while the Nazis were setting up a ghetto in the brick factory called the [INAUDIBLE] Teglagyar.
That means-- [INAUDIBLE] was the name of the owner.
Teglagyar means brick factory.
And there they amassed thousands and thousands of Jews from the entire province of Berehy [INAUDIBLE].
End of tape two.
